kept.text:
หุ้นเล็กมันไม่ใช่แค่เรื่องความรู้ว่าบริษัททำอะไร ดีแค่ไหน หรืองบเป็นยังไง เพราะเรื่องพวกนั้นใครๆ ก็ศึกษาได้หมด แต่สิ่งที่ยากจริงๆ คือ “จิตใจ” ถ้าคุณยังหวั่นไหวไปกับราคาที่ขึ้นลงทุกวัน คุณจะไม่มีทางถือหุ้นดีๆ ไปจนถึงวันที่มันโตจริงได้เลย

อย่าง $RKLB ของผม กว่าจะได้ +1000% มันไม่ได้มาง่ายๆ ระหว่างทางผมผ่านช่วงที่ติดลบหนักๆ มาแล้ว รวมถึง $PLTR ด้วย แต่ผมยังถืออยู่ เพราะผมเชื่อในสิ่งที่ผมลงทุน และผมทำการบ้านมาดีพอ เวลาหุ้นลงมันเลยไม่ได้น่ากลัวขนาดนั้นสำหรับผม

ผมห้ามไม่ให้ใครซื้อตามผมไม่ได้หรอกครับ แต่ผมอยากเตือน เพราะไม่อยากให้ใครเอาเงินมาเสี่ยงแล้วสุดท้ายทนไม่ไหวแล้วขายทิ้ง 

ถ้าคุณรู้ตัวว่ายังรับความผันผวนแบบนี้ไม่ได้ หุ้นใหญ่ก็เป็นทางเลือกที่ดี ค่อยๆ ลงทุน เติมไปเรื่อยๆ อยู่ในตลาดให้นานพอ สุดท้ายพอร์ตมันโตเองจริงๆ

สำหรับผม เป้าหมายมันชัดอยู่แล้ว วันนึงถ้าพอร์ตถึงจุดที่ต้องการ ผมก็จะย้ายไปหุ้นผู้นำตลาด ไม่ต้องหวือหวา แต่เน้นความมั่นคงและการเติบโตระยะยาว เพราะสุดท้ายแล้ว เกมนี้มันไม่ได้วัดว่าใครวิ่งเร็วที่สุด แต่วัดว่าใครอยู่รอดได้นานที่สุด 🚀

profile_bio.description: Long-term investor | MBA, M.Econ | วิเคราะห์หุ้นจากพื้นฐาน ลงทุนใน Megatrend อนาคต | ผมแชร์ทุกข้อมูลที่ไม่มีในนี้ ไว้ใน Ko-fi ลองเข้าไปส่องกันได้ 👇🏻

quoted_tweet.text:
หุ้น 10 เด้ง ที่ผมเจอ มันจะมี “DNA” หรือความเหมือนกันอยู่

หุ้นที่ไม่ได้แค่โตเร็ว แต่กำลังสร้างโลกอุตสาหกรรมใหม่ของตัวเอง

จากหุ้นที่ผมถือจริงอย่าง $RKLB และ $PLTR มันคล้ายๆ กับเส้นทางของ Tesla หรือ Nvidia ในยุคแรกๆ เลย คือมันไม่ได้สวยงามเลยในช่วงแรก ไม่มีคนเข้าใจว่าบริษัททำอะไร จะมีอนาคตมั้ย? อย่าง $RKLB ที่มีเจ้าตลาดอย่าง SpaceX อยู่แล้ว จะสู้ได้ยังไง แต่ถ้า CEO ไม่กล้าคิด ก็จะเป็นแค่หุ้นกระจอกๆ ตัวนึง แต่นี่ CEO กล้าคิดกล้าทำ มันเลยสร้างการเปลี่ยนแปลงได้

ผมเขียนบทความนี้ไว้เพื่อจะเป็นกรณีศึกษา ว่าผมซื้อหุ้น 2 ตัวนี้โดยมี Mindset ยังไง และมีระบบในการถืออย่างไรให้ได้ 10 เด้ง ไม่ใช่แค่หลับหูหลับตาถือ เพราะหุ้นในพอร์ตผมยังมีอีกหลายตัวมีที่โอกาสจะไปถึง 10 เด้ง และผมก็จะไม่หยุดแค่ 10 เด้งแน่นอน เป้าหมายผมคือ 100 เด้ง ผมเลยจำเป็นต้องมีแผนหรือระบบไว้รองรับการตัดสินใจของผมด้วย

เนื้อหาบทความจะครอบคลุมทั้งวิธีมองหุ้น 10 เด้ง, Mindset ที่ผมใช้ถือยาว, และ ระบบการถือของผม

อ่านจบจะเข้าใจเลยว่า หุ้น 10 เด้งไม่ได้เกิดจากโชค แต่เกิดจาก “ความเข้าใจ + ความอดทน + ระบบที่ดี”

ใครอยากอ่านไปได้ที่นี่เลยครับ ผมเปิดให้อ่านฟรีเลย: https://t.co/WtihN8wJQt

SpaceX가 AI와 직접 엮이면서, 일론이 앵커 역할로 상당한 노이즈를 만들 때 

시장에서는 순수 우주 기업의 대안이 전혀 없는 상태.

$RKLB 을 기관이 좋아하는 이유는 단 하나.

기업이 존나 탄탄함. 

경영진부터, 운영 방식 등등

$RKLB $TSLA $PLTR

quoted_tweet.text:
Here's a bunch of random 30 US-available random stocks I like today and why:  

1. $INTC - America's hope for foundry, national security
2. $MRVL - scales rev from future maia asics and add ons like cpo, they do everything lost count
3. $TSM - backbone of semis/ai
4. $COHR - They do everything vertically integrated + captures optical cycle
5. $RKLB - the final frontier of space will be around 5 years from now and 20 years from now. 
6. $DRAM - memory exposure for samsung/sk hynix
7. $AVGO - hyperscalers dont like nvidia gpu tax
8. $AMZN - nobody can compete against the overnight shipping of toilet paper. robotics will lower opex over time
9. $ARM - AGI CPUs scale revenue quite a bit over the next decade
10. $TSEM - you're going to need a foundry for light based stuff
11. $IBIT - bitcoin, we all know by now
12. $NBIS - i think it's the next AWS. Also they do self-driving cars with uber, own scaling DB companies, data labeling. It's almost like a mini Google. 
13. $GOOGL - youtube is not going away, gemini is great. they're vertically integrated with TPUs and fund buildout with operating income so i like it. 
14. $AMKR - super facilities coming online in late 2027-2028. benefits from made in america
15. $HOOD - i dont like short term, but long term i'm a fan of Robinhood since they captured retail + have more products like banking, etc that they're scaling up. product innovation is wild.
16. $CRCL - I happen to really like stablecoins and see them as the future for both payments/holding (depends on clarity act)
17. $META - people aren't going to stop using instagram or whatsapp, or others anytime soon. 
18. $LITE - $GOOGL TPU exposure decently high part of BOM. As long as Google's AI program keeps running I think $LITE will do well.
19. $LPTH - Germanium and China export controls will always be an issue so US made engineered alternatives  will always be important
20. $FN - Someone needs to assemble optical stuff
21. $JBL - same as above, but added with ip from Intel's SiPh acqusition so might end up like innolight?
22. $MP - American rare earths program is extremely important, similar to $INTC national security risks
23. $HIMS - Okay here me out they just acquired a ton of companies, and at $19 they have global DTC channel. short sellers really hate this company, but I think it's actually promising as a contrarian long
24. $SMTC - LRO/LPO transition
25. $POWL - US alternative to hammond for switchgear DC type bottleneck
26. $VPG - Humanoids will be a thing down the road maybe 2027-2028, this makes the sensors. 
27. $MOG.A - Feels like i see them everywhere in robotics, to spacex supply chains
28. $MSFT - At $375, one day we'll look back and see this as a buying opportunity.
29. $CVX - oil might crash after war but these oil companies are going to be extremely important, especially when Venezulea is a goldmine.
30. $XLU - i think rate cuts might be back online, we need power/grid for AI so these names will always be improtant from $CEG to $NEE

Just throwing out other thoughts aside from $AAOI and $AEHR.

kept.text:
$rklb i keep coming back to rocket lab because the electron story is easy to underestimate.

it is not the biggest rocket.
it is not the loudest narrative in space.
it does not have the same cultural gravity as starship.

but that may be exactly why it is interesting.

rklb is building a business around something much less glamorous:

showing up, launching often, and putting specific payloads into specific orbits for customers who care about schedule more than spectacle.

the latest little signal on my feed is the rumored “viva la strix” mission patch showing up around rocket lab / synspective.

i would not treat a merch-store patch as official confirmation by itself.

but i do think it fits a larger pattern that is already visible:

synspective keeps coming back to rocket lab.

that is the part i care about.

not the patch.
not the owl jokes.
although the owl economy is apparently alive and well.

synspective is building a sar constellation.

sar matters because it can image the earth through clouds, at night, and in conditions where regular optical imagery struggles.

that makes it useful for infrastructure monitoring, disaster response, maritime awareness, construction, agriculture, insurance, and eventually a lot of government use cases.

this is not just “another small satellite.”

it is a data business that needs repeatable access to orbit.

this is where electron has a real niche.

a constellation operator does not always want to wait for a rideshare slot.

sometimes it wants a specific orbit.
sometimes it wants control over timing.
sometimes it wants fewer compromises around deployment.
sometimes it wants to build the constellation step by step with a launch partner that already understands the spacecraft.

that is where dedicated small launch still has a job.

what i like about the synspective relationship is the repetition.

one launch is a transaction.
two launches are a customer.
eight launches start to look like trust.
a larger multi-launch agreement starts to look like infrastructure.

that is the part of rocket lab i think people sometimes miss.

the business is not only about the rocket.

it is about becoming a reliable operating layer for customers building real space businesses.

there is also a second-order effect here.

every repeat customer reduces friction.

the teams know the spacecraft.
the integration process gets cleaner.
mission design becomes less novel.
risk gets easier to price.
the customer can plan around the launch provider instead of shopping for a new solution every time.

that is not a flashy moat.

but in aerospace, boring repeatability can become a very real advantage.

the market often wants one clean answer for rklb:

is it a launch company?
is it a space systems company?
is it a defense contractor?
is it a neutron option?

i think the better way to look at it is this:

electron is the trust engine.

it gets customers into the ecosystem.
it proves reliability.
it creates relationships.
it gives rocket lab a front-row seat to what constellation operators actually need.

that matters because the best space companies may not be the ones that only sell one product.

they may be the ones that move up and down the stack.

launch.
spacecraft.
components.
software.
operations.
data infrastructure.
defense programs.

electron may look small in isolation, but it gives rocket lab a real operating surface across the industry.

so when i see another synspective signal, i do not read it as just “another electron launch.”

i read it as another data point in the same direction:

rocket lab is becoming a repeat supplier to customers building durable space infrastructure.

that is a different kind of story from a one-off launch headline.

less dramatic.
more compound.

for me, the question is not whether electron can compete with every rocket in every market.

it does not need to.

the question is whether rocket lab can keep turning reliability, cadence, and customer intimacy into more work across the full space stack.

that is the part i am watching with rklb.

not because every mission changes the company overnight.

but because enough repeat missions can change what the company is.

kept.text:
$RKLB

Interesting. More buyers coming in now?

I don't usually put much weight on technical analysis, but this actually made a lot of sense to me:

"You want to see that buying support come in. And Rocket Lab had it in spades coming in right kind of at the bottom of that gap up day, right at that horizontal line, right at the 21 day moving average line. So you have all these different support levels [...] a lot of different strategies looking at the same level and saying, hey, this is where I would want to add to my position or start a position or protect my position."

kept.text:
$RKLB (Algo Flow) - Algos finding a bottom?

Last week, net options activity fell off a cliff. Flow dropped from near zero all the way to -4M while price slid from $92 to $78. By Friday, both were recovering — flow climbed back to -2.5M and price bounced to $82. The selling slowed. It hasn't stopped.

Here's the important part: Flow is still well below zero and that's the line that matters. The bounce is real but unconfirmed. 

Catalyst stack remains strong: CEO cut salary to $1, Neutron launch permit filed for H2 2026, $190M DoD hypersonic contract.

Bulls watch: flow continuing to climb toward zero — if it crosses above with price reclaiming $87+, the bearish read flips entirely.

profile_bio.description: I enjoy drinking good wine as much as I do trading stocks; they also happen to both cost me money.

quoted_tweet.text:
$RKLB (Max Pain $80 | Price $79) — May 1 expiry

1.2M in calls at $125 and price is $1 under the pin. The ladder is fully loaded.

Calls start building at $82, stay measured through $100, then accelerate hard from $104 to $125 where every strike is measured in millions. 

Price sitting just under max pain with 5 days left means positioning gravity is already pulling upward.

kept.text:
$rklb

the obvious rklb angle from meta’s space solar news is launch.

big orbital power systems need to get to space.

fair.

but i think the more interesting angle is not launch first.

it is the power supply chain itself.

if space solar becomes real, the hard question becomes:

who can manufacture space-qualified power hardware at scale?

meta’s new partnership with overview energy is aiming at up to 1 gw of space solar capacity.

overview’s concept is to collect solar energy in geosynchronous orbit, then beam it down to existing solar farms as low-intensity near-infrared light.

that is not a small satellite hobby project.

if it works, it becomes an industrial power system in orbit.

and industrial systems need industrial suppliers.

this is where rocket lab becomes hard to ignore.

rocket lab is already in the space solar business through its solar cells, solar panels, substrates, power modules, and solar array wings.

this is not the part of the company that gets the most memes.

but it may be one of the most strategically important parts.

what i like about rklb here is the vertical integration.

rocket lab says it offers solar cells, solar cell assemblies, solar modules, solar panel substrates, complete solar panels, and entire solar array wings under one roof.

that matters because space power is not a commodity panel you grab from a warehouse.

it has to survive radiation, thermal cycling, vibration, launch loads, and years of operation.

space is rude to hardware.

there is also a materials angle.

traditional space solar often uses gallium arsenide and germanium because they perform well in harsh radiation environments.

but those materials can be expensive and supply-constrained.

rocket lab recently introduced advanced silicon solar arrays aimed at constellation-scale production and space-based data centers.

that is the part i keep circling.

if space power scales, cost per watt matters.

mass matters.
stowage matters.
deployment matters.
manufacturing throughput matters.
supply chain resilience matters.

this is not just about having the highest-efficiency cell in a lab.

it is about making power systems that can be produced, launched, deployed, and operated at industrial scale.

that is a very different game.

this is why i do not want to frame rklb only as “a rocket company.”

that misses the more interesting structure.

electron gives cadence.
neutron may add medium-lift optionality.
space systems adds components, spacecraft, and power hardware.
mynaric adds optical communications.

the company is slowly building around the infrastructure layer of space.

the meta announcement makes that infrastructure layer more valuable in my mind.

because meta is not buying a space story for fun.

it is trying to solve a data center energy problem.

that means the demand signal is coming from outside the traditional space market.

when non-space customers start needing space hardware, the category gets more interesting.

this is the part i think investors should be careful with, though.

a demand signal is not the same thing as a contract.

rklb is not automatically the winner of every space solar project.

overview energy has its own architecture, partners, suppliers, and timeline.

there will be technical risk, regulatory risk, cost risk, and execution risk.

i would not turn one headline into a full thesis.

but i would absolutely update the mental model.

before this, space solar could be treated as a niche future idea.

after this, one of the largest ai infrastructure buyers is publicly reserving capacity from a space solar system.

that does not prove the market.

but it does make the market harder to ignore.

rklb benefits from that kind of category formation.

not because it has to own the final customer relationship.

sometimes the better business is being the supplier behind multiple architectures.

power hardware.
spacecraft components.
mission systems.
launch.
eventually, maybe neutron for larger deployments.

picks and shovels can be boring.

boring is not always bad.

i also think this connects to space-based data centers.

rocket lab specifically talked about silicon solar arrays for gigawatt-scale space-based data centers.

meta is talking about space solar for earth-based data centers.

different endpoint.

same underlying issue:

ai needs more power, and orbital infrastructure is becoming part of the conversation.

that is the bridge.

there is a funny little inversion here.

for decades, space companies needed terrestrial customers to fund space infrastructure.

now ai companies may need space infrastructure to keep scaling terrestrial compute.

that is a strange sentence.

but it is exactly why this news matters.

the near-term rklb impact may be mostly narrative.

the medium-term impact could be customer conversations.

the long-term impact, if the category works, could be a much larger tam for space power systems than the market was giving credit for.

that is the chain i am watching.

not immediate revenue.
category validation first.
supplier relevance second.
financial impact later.

so my rklb takeaway is pretty simple.

i am not buying the idea that this one meta announcement proves everything.

but i do think it strengthens the case that space power is becoming a serious infrastructure category.

and if that happens, rocket lab’s solar manufacturing heritage may matter a lot more than the market currently models.

kept.text:
$rklb the mynaric piece of the rocket lab story is more important than it looks at first glance.

it is easy to file it under “another acquisition.”

but i do not think that is the right mental model.

rocket lab is not just adding a component.

it is adding a deeper position in the space communications stack.

and that stack may become one of the most important parts of the next space economy.

launch gets the attention because rockets are visual.

you can watch them.
you can count them.
you can feel the drama.

but once satellites are in orbit, the harder question is:

how do they move data?

between satellites.
down to earth.
across military networks.
across commercial constellations.
with low latency.
with resilience.
with security.

that is where optical communications starts to matter.

mynaric builds laser communication terminals.

in simple terms, these systems help satellites talk to each other using optical links instead of relying only on traditional radio frequency paths.

the reason this matters is not just speed.

it is network design.

if leo becomes a dense operating environment, satellites cannot just be isolated assets.

they need to become nodes in a network.

that is especially relevant for defense space.

modern defense is moving toward proliferated constellations, faster sensor-to-shooter loops, missile warning, tracking, communications, and distributed resilience.

i do not see this as a sci-fi story.

i see it as a very practical infrastructure story.

more satellites create more data.
more data requires better transport.
better transport requires terminals, links, standards, and integration.

this is where rocket lab’s direction gets interesting.

the company already has launch.
it has spacecraft manufacturing.
it has components.
it has national security exposure.
it has sda-related work.
and now it has a stronger optical communications terminal capability inside the house.

that does not make execution easy.

but it does make the company harder to describe as just a small launch provider.

i also think this fits the vertical integration pattern.

rocket lab has spent years trying to control more of the stack.

not because vertical integration sounds good in a slide deck.

but because space hardware is full of bottlenecks.

if a supplier becomes a constraint, your schedule becomes someone else’s problem.
if a component is critical, your margin can leak out of the system.
if the government customer wants speed, integration risk matters.

owning more of the stack can be painful, but it can also become a strategic advantage.

the mynaric deal is not risk-free.

turnarounds are hard.
production scaling is hard.
defense programs are demanding.
optical terminals are not magic boxes.
and acquisitions can distract companies if integration is poor.

so i would not treat this as an automatic win.

but i do think the direction makes sense.

rocket lab is trying to own more of the architecture, not just deliver hardware into it.

this is also why i keep thinking about rklb less like a pure launch trade and more like a space systems compounder.

launch is still important.
neutron is still important.
electron still matters.

but the deeper question is whether rocket lab can become a supplier of critical infrastructure across multiple layers of space.

rockets get things to orbit.
space systems keep the business expanding after the launch.

there is a funny part of this story too.

the public wants rockets because rockets are cool.

but the real money may end up hiding in the “boring” pieces:

terminals.
reaction wheels.
star trackers.
solar.
separation systems.
software.
integration.
ground systems.
optical links.

it is always the unsexy plumbing that eventually becomes the expensive plumbing.

so my mynaric take is pretty simple.

i am not watching it because i think every acquisition deserves applause.

i am watching it because it points to a larger strategy:

rocket lab wants to be closer to the nervous system of space infrastructure.

not just the taxi.
not just the bus.
not just the factory.

but part of the network layer that lets satellites actually do useful work.

that is a much bigger idea than “small rocket company.”

MUSK PAY TIED TO MARS COLONY AND SPACE DATA CENTERS

SpaceX has tied Elon Musk’s pay to bold goals like building a Mars colony and space-based data centers. He could earn massive share awards if the company hits a $7.5 trillion valuation and establishes a million-person presence on Mars, along with major computing capacity in orbit.

He gets nothing unless these targets are met and currently earns only a minimal salary. The plan is highly unusual and may create tension with Tesla, as both companies compete for his attention.
